source: BG2 Pod — SpaceX IPO, AI capex, Cursor
summary: AI compute monetization rates per gigawatt are increasing, not deflating, contrary to consensus expectations entering 2026
summary_extended: Baker endorses a structural view that AI compute monetization rates per gigawatt are rising sharply, not deflating as consensus expected entering 2026, with Fox citing an increase from roughly $20B/GW at the start of the year to $30–40B/GW currently. The core reasoning is that token pricing is moving up, not down, because demand is outstripping supply — a dynamic Baker attributes in part to the extremely low current penetration of agentic AI use (less than 0.2% of people use AI agentically), meaning any meaningful scaling of that use case implies a sustained and prolonged compute shortage. Baker explicitly endorses owning asset-heavy businesses — hyperscalers, power infrastructure, and data center operators — in this inflationary token-pricing environment, arguing that the scarcity dynamic rewards capital-intensive incumbents. The investment implication is to be long the physical infrastructure stack (power, data centers, networking) that benefits from rising revenue-per-watt, rather than assuming commoditization of AI compute.
detail: Fox reports monetization rates rising from ~$20B/GW at start of year to $30-40B/GW now. Token pricing is going up, not down, as demand outstrips supply. Less than 0.2% of people use AI agentically; scaling to any meaningful percentage implies sustained shortage. Baker endorses owning asset-heavy businesses in inflationary token-pricing environments.
confidence_evidence: Specific data points on monetization rate increase cited; Fox presents as direct market observation; Baker explicitly endorses the inflationary framing
quote_fragment: monetization rates per watt are increasing

ticker_audit.reasoning: NVDA is the dominant supplier of GPU compute that underpins the constrained supply side of this thesis. Rising monetization rates per GW and sustained compute shortage directly benefit NVDA as the primary hardware vendor capturing pricing power in a supply-constrained market. [RE-AUDIT: Baker's thesis is about AI compute monetization rates per gigawatt rising for asset-heavy infrastructure owners. NVDA is a GPU supplier — it is tangential to this specific thesis about revenue-per-GW for infrastructure operators. Baker did not specifically name NVDA as a beneficiary of rising monetization rates per GW in this thesis. The supply constraint dynamic benefits infrastructure owners, not necessarily the chip vendor. Remove per the rule: if Baker said 'Nvidia' in a different context in the same interview, it does not belong here.]

Vistra is a power generator that directly benefits from rising demand for electricity driven by AI data center buildout; Baker's thesis on rising monetization per GW and sustained compute shortage implies sustained power demand growth, making power generators a clear asset-heavy beneficiary.
